subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: 4 starting tiles (single-square kingdom centers); 4 3D castles (1 pink, 1 yellow, 1 green, 1 blue); 48 dominoes (each domino has 1 landscape side and 1 numbered side); 8 wooden kings in 4 colors (2 pink, 2 yellow, 2 green, 2 blue). Before first play, assemble the 4 castles.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: Kingdomino has no shared central map board, but there is a shared draft display near the game box. 1) Place the game box as the draw area as shown in the rulebook. 2) Dominoes are stored in the box with number sides visible to form the draw pile. 3) During setup and each round, reveal a line of dominoes next to the box in ascending numerical order (lowest number closest to the box), then flip to landscape side. 4) Each player builds their personal kingdom in front of them, centered around their starting tile and constrained to a 5x5 area.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player chooses a color and takes matching pieces: in 3- or 4-player games, take 1 king of that color; in 2-player games, take both kings of that color (2 kings each). Each player also takes a starting tile and matching-color castle, places the starting tile face up in front of them, and puts the castle on it. This tile is the central anchor for that player’s kingdom.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: Prepare domino pool by player count: 2 players use 24 random dominoes (set aside 24); 3 players use 36 random dominoes (set aside 12); 4 players use all 48. Draw a number of dominoes equal to kings in play (3 in a 3-player game; 4 in a 2- or 4-player game). Arrange them in ascending number order (lowest nearest the box), then flip to landscape side. For the first turn only, one player takes all kings in hand, randomly reveals them one at a time, and each revealed king is placed on any free domino in that row (so king color may not match owner choice order). One king per domino. In 2-player, each player places both kings and therefore claims two dominoes. Once all kings are placed, reveal a second line from the draw pile using the same ascending-order method. At this point there are two rows: current claimed row (with kings) and next available row (without kings).

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content: Kingdomino is played in ordered turns based on king position in the claimed row of dominoes. Use this sequence each turn: 1) Determine order from lowest-numbered claimed domino to highest. 2) Active king’s player resolves D1: take the domino bearing that king and place it into their kingdom following all connection rules and the 5x5 limit. 3) Same player resolves D2: place that king onto one domino in the next row to claim it for a future turn. 4) Continue in order until all kings in the claimed row have resolved D1 and D2. 5) Reveal a new row from the draw pile in ascending order and flip to landscape side; this becomes the new next row. 6) Start the next turn using order determined by kings on the newly claimed row. In a 2-player game, each player performs D1 and D2 twice per turn (once with each king). Game length: 12 turns in 3- or 4-player games; 6 turns in 2-player games (because each player acts with two kings each turn).

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content: On each king activation, a player has two mandatory action steps in order: D1 Place claimed domino, then D2 Claim a new domino. D1 placement rules: a) Domino must fit inside a personal 5x5 kingdom boundary (counting all squares, including starting tile). b) Domino must connect either to the starting tile (starting tile is wild and may connect to any terrain) OR to another placed domino by matching at least one terrain type. c) Terrain matching for legal connection is orthogonal only (horizontal/vertical), not diagonal. d) If a domino cannot be legally placed (connection fails or 5x5 boundary would be exceeded), it is discarded and scores 0 at endgame. D2 draft rule: place your king on one unclaimed domino in the next row; one king per domino. Your selected domino determines your future turn order because lower numbers act first in the next turn. There is no resource cost to placing or claiming; the core constraints are geometry, terrain matching, and turn-order timing.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content: The central mechanism is initiative drafting: the domino you pick now determines both tile quality and your next-turn position. Lower-number dominoes usually grant earlier turn order next round, while higher numbers often tempt with better terrain/crown combinations but delay your next pick. This creates a rolling tension between immediate board value and tempo control. Another key system is hard spatial confinement: your kingdom must end within exactly a 5x5 frame, so orientation and growth direction are strategic from the opening turns.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content: End trigger: when the last dominoes are lined up, players play one final turn using only D1 placement from already claimed dominoes (no further D2 claiming). Then score all kingdoms. Scoring method: 1) Divide your kingdom into TERRITORIES: connected groups (orthogonally connected) of the same terrain type. 2) For each territory, score = number of squares in that territory × number of crowns in that same territory. 3) A territory with zero crowns scores 0 points, even if large. 4) Total score is the sum of all territory scores. Highest total wins. Tiebreakers: first, player with the single biggest territory by square count wins; if tied, player with the most crowns wins; if still tied, tied players share victory. Official optional rule variants (if used) add scoring sources: The Middle Kingdom grants +10 points if your castle is in the exact center of your territory; Harmony grants +5 points if your kingdom is complete with no discarded dominoes; Dynasty is a 3-game series where highest cumulative points wins; The Mighty Duel (2 players) uses all dominoes and a 7x7 kingdom format.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: 1) Build a flexible shape around your starting tile, not a narrow corridor. Keep multiple edges open so future domino shapes still fit under the 5x5 cap. 2) Prioritize at least one early crown in a terrain you expect to expand; crowns multiply area, so a crowned region can scale fast. 3) Watch initiative while drafting: sometimes taking a slightly weaker tile to act earlier next turn gives access to critical crowns or terrain connections. 4) In 2-player games, coordinate both kings as one plan—use one king for position control (turn order) and the other for value growth. 5) Place with final boundaries in mind from turn one: mentally reserve corners and edges for awkward domino orientations so you avoid late forced discards.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content: 1) Expanding without crowns. New players often chase big same-terrain zones but forget that zero crowns means zero points regardless of size. 2) Ignoring the 5x5 boundary until late game. A strong tile that breaks your footprint can become a dead draw and must be discarded. 3) Drafting purely by tile strength and forgetting turn order. Taking a high-number tile can hand opponents first pick of the next key row. 4) Over-fragmenting terrain into many tiny regions. Separate 2–3 tile regions with one crown usually lose to one larger merged region with the same crown total. 5) Wasting starter-tile flexibility. The starting tile connects to any terrain; failing to use it as a universal bridge can lock your layout too early.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content: 1) Tempo vs value: Do you claim a lower-number domino for first choice next turn, or a higher-number domino with stronger crowns now? This is the core draft tension every turn. 2) Consolidate vs diversify: Should you extend one major territory to maximize crown multiplication, or build secondary regions to hedge against awkward future drafts? 3) Immediate fit vs future geometry: A domino that fits perfectly now may close essential expansion lanes; sometimes a modest placement that preserves options is strategically better than a short-term point gain.

metadata.notes: Primary source: provided Kingdomino rulebook excerpt (EN/FR/DE sections). Core game fully covered; optional official variants noted in endgame.
